title: WordPiece
description: A subword tokenizer that scores candidate merges by likelihood, famously used in BERT-family encoders.
openingSummary: WordPiece builds subword tokens by greedily choosing splits that maximize language-model likelihood, which is why BERT checkpoints expose ##-prefixed continuation pieces in their vocabularies.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: WordPiece selects subword units by maximizing the probability of a training corpus under a unigram language model, then applies greedy longest-match tokenization at inference. BERT and many encoder-only transformers ship WordPiece vocabularies with continuation markers on non-initial pieces.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Encoder APIs and embedding products still run WordPiece tokenizers daily. Mis-identifying a checkpoint as BPE leads to wrong vocab files and broken special-token handling. WordPiece also influences how punctuation attaches to words, which changes effective context length for the same character count.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: "playing" might tokenize as ["play", "##ing"] in a BERT vocabulary, where ## marks a continuation subword rather than a standalone token.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: WordPiece is not identical to BPE even though both emit subwords—merge criteria differ. It is also not the Unigram algorithm, though both use probabilistic scoring; SentencePiece can implement Unigram but uses different surface conventions than classic WordPiece files.
